hypothesis: Certain pairs of stocks share a long-run equilibrium relationship (cointegration). When the spread between them deviates beyond a threshold, it reverts. This is market-neutral by construction.
universe: Same-sector pairs with fundamental linkage (e.g., Coke/Pepsi, GM/Ford). Screen via cointegration tests.
entry_logic: Calculate the spread's z-score. Enter long the underperformer and short the outperformer when z-score exceeds +/- entry threshold (typically 2.0).
exit_logic: Exit when z-score returns to 0 (or close to it). Stop-loss if z-score exceeds a wider threshold (e.g., 3.5), indicating possible cointegration breakdown.
position_sizing: Hedge ratio from the cointegration regression (beta-adjusted). Dollar-neutral.
risk_management: Re-test cointegration monthly. If p-value > 0.05, close the pair. Max holding period 30 days. Portfolio of 10-20 pairs for diversification.
known_failure_modes: Cointegration breakdown: pairs can lose their relationship permanently (M&A, sector shifts).
Survivorship bias: backtests on historical pairs that survived look better than reality.
Short borrowing costs eat into thin spreads.
Requires large pair universe to diversify idiosyncratic risk.

historical_performance.notes: Very stable when it works. Performance varies heavily by pair selection methodology.
